author: @JasonSheehan-fy3lb
text: Use polar array to create the circular pattern ..set the first circle at the distance from the centre constrain it to the vertical axis , select the circle select polar array, select circular array, enter the number of circles you need .. and your done..

author: @NicBellamy
text:
Centering a square or rectangle around the origin can be done much quicker (and with far fewer fixed dimensions) in Sketcher in two ways:

Method one: click the little down arrow next to the square tool to find the "centered rectangle" tool, then hover over the origin until the mouse pointer has an "X" coincident constraint showing, click to place it, then pull away to make and dimension your square, nicely pinned around the origin.

Method two: draw your square as you did in the video, then, starting with nothing selected, hold ctrl and select two diagonally opposite corners of the square,  then select the origin point, then click the "symmetry constraint". With diagonal corners symmetrical around the origin, it's guaranteed to be centered. Symmetry constraints have far more uses than just squares too.

For your fillets, dimension one of them, then use the "equals" constraint to make the others equal to it. Quicker,  and only one place to change the radius in future.

I highly recommend MangoJelly's FreeCAD videos if you haven't already found them.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - but method 1 is exactly what I did

replies.author: @NicBellamy
replies.text: At 14:40 and 23:00 you made the square then pinned it to the origin with half the width and half the height dimensions - which is why I thought to point out some quicker options. All three ways are a valid path to "done" though, just trying to pass on some quality of life tips I've picked up 🙂

author: @EUPThatsMe
text: Could draw the center circle and the one above, on the axis, then pocket that and use a polar copy in the 3D view to get 6 evenly spaced holes around the middle - Save yourself some headache and set the "Auto Remove Redundant" constraints check box in "Edit|Preferences|Sketcher General" and unset the "Notify automatic constraint substitutions" check boxs - Center rectangle is your friend

author: @brandonmack111
text:
This advice seems applicable to almost anything in life.

Obviously some more than others - you won't learn brain surgery by just getting up and trying it out , for example, but even a neurosurgeon, at some point, must perform actually brain surgeries before they can truly become proficient at it.

For most things in life, though, the best way to learn is to do. Learn the basics, then jump right in. Art, science, even relationships, and parenthood - you can't really learn to do by reading about it, and you'll never be good at it until you actually get out there and start. You'll learn and improve along the way.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: absolutely mate - cheers

author: @ewjorgy
text:
Hi Robert, 

There is a very useful feature in sketch that allows you to use construction lines. These are lines that are simply reference lines that won't show up in the model. That line in the first sketch that you deleted could have been left there if you changed it to a construction line. 

Also, if you select a face of an object and then hit sketch it will create the sketch on that face. Super useful!

Anyways, just a heads up so you know it's there. Hope it helps! 

Take care!

replies.author: @CallistoPili
replies.text: yes, by doing it as a hexagon you get it done in a minute :)

replies.author: @chris993361
replies.text: ​@CallistoPiliGood thinking. I would have used a polar array and it would not have occurred to me to just use a hexagon. Certainly a million ways to do something

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate nice tips

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That's not the point mate - If you are going to give folks a tip make it more useful if you can - a bit more of a pointer - this video isn't meant for wizards of CAD but for absolute beginners - the comments also help people learn but only if you help out with making your suggestion a bit more of a pointer - a few people have mentioned your suggestion but with enough detail to follow - it would have been a great comment if you had done that

replies.author: @chris993361
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkeringI'm not sure which of the comments specifically you were referring to. If it's the construction line comment, if while you were in the sketch you select the line, there is a toggle in your toolbar that will change it to construction geometry. This will turn it into a dash line that will get ignored when you extrude the sketch. But that line can still be used as a constraint for the circle. To add another tip to that part of the video, instead of doing a zero dimension to an axis, you can click the center of the circle and click the axis and you have a coincidence or constrain on object button in your toolbar you can click that will lock the center of the circle to the axis.

author: @AldoInza
text:
You can use symmetry on the vertices and go counter-corner on a box to center it around the origin point vertex. You can do the same for creating symmetrical holes. It becomes rotationally symmetrical when you go symmetrical to a point.

Also check out STEP files when generating something in FreeCad, because it saves it as geometry which can be easily edited, as opposed to stl which is actually a bunch of high resolution triangles. This makes a difference because you make so many round parts, which STEP preserves.

replies.text: cheers mate
